pattern: An imbalance number is just the number of gaps between consecutive distinct values present -- one less than the count of 'runs' of consecutive integers -- so inserting one new value at a time and tracking how it merges or extends a run updates the total without ever re-sorting.
hints: The imbalance number of a set of values only cares about which distinct values are present, not how many times each one appears, so track presence per value rather than the actual multiset.

Fix the left end of a subarray and grow the right end one element at a time, keeping a 'seen' marker per value; adding a brand-new value that already has both a left and a right neighbor present merges two runs into one, adding one with no neighbor present starts a new run, and one with exactly one neighbor present just extends an existing run.
After each extension, add the current window's imbalance number to a running total, then reset the seen-markers and repeat for the next starting left index -- an O(n^2) sweep is fast enough here.

edge_inputs.description: three overlapping subarrays each contribute imbalance 1
several subarrays including one with imbalance 2
single element, no subarray can be imbalanced
all identical values, always zero imbalance
already sorted consecutive run, always zero imbalance
reverse-sorted consecutive run, still always zero imbalance
inserting a value that bridges two existing runs must reduce the count, not add to it
duplicates interleaved with a gap should not be double-counted

stress.description: 1000-length array (max length), values within the required 1..n range
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 1000))
    nums = [rng.randint(1, n) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums]
